free_text: Cross section of radioactive isotopes formation in a
tin target irradiated by 660 MeV protons
- prelim. data for Sn
Final (combined) publication
(in Russian), see REL-REF
English Transl. of IZV,59,206
-prelim.publ.of IZV,59,(5),206
- cr.sect. of Ag-isotope
- cr.sect. of Ti,V,Ga,Ge isotopes
- cr.sect. of Zr isotopes
- cr.sect. of Mo isotopes
Two Sn-targets, thickness 0.132 g/cm2,d=30 mm were used
Ge(Li) detectors 38 and 50 cm2 in volume with an
energy resolution of 2.5 keV for Egamma ~ 1.3 MeV.
Two tin targets with a diameter of 30 mm and a
thickness of 0.132 were irradiated, one for 10 minutes,
the other for 1 hour. The proton flux density
(respectively 1.08 x 10**12 and 1.13 x 10**12 /cm2/s)
is determined by the activity of 24Na in aluminum
monitors of the same diameter and thickness of
0.025 g/cm, where cross section is taken to be equal
to 10.8 mb.
The spectra of unseparated reaction products
were measured after 50 min. Each target was exposed
65 times over 5 months.
The error in the calibration of spectrometer.
Experimental errors of the measurements,
which do not include errors of the gamma-yields,
half-lives and monitor reaction cross section.
Table 4
The table shows the weighted average cross section
values ??of two independent experiments.
'Reference' has been corrected.
Flags and MISC-COL are added to data
section. MONITOR and METHOD were corrected.
Small correction in reference.
Elem/Mass formalism was transformed to
multiply reaction formalism.
Last checking has been done.

100% Isomer transition. (ENSDF)
-By Compiler- The authors assumed 58. min activity as
In-108m activity. But according to ENSDF the activity
is ground state decay activity. Isomer transition is
absent.
In-108g production due to radioactive decay of Sn-108
is negligible.
SD: SF5=IND deleted from REACTION code.
-By Compiler- Original report and combined paper do not
inform a reader what state of Cd-115 has been observed.
Cd-115 has long-lived (T1/2=44.6D) isomer state.
Compiler assumed 2.23 days activity as ground state
activity, according to ENSDF.
